sections.heading: What is a Broadcast Receiver?
sections.content: A Broadcast Receiver is an Android component that allows your app to subscribe to and respond to system-wide or app-specific broadcast messages. Broadcasts are asynchronous messages sent by the Android system or other apps when events of interest occur — the battery is low, the network connectivity changes, the device boots, a file download completes, or your app sends a custom event. A BroadcastReceiver's onReceive() callback is called on the main thread and must complete quickly — within approximately 10 seconds on most devices — otherwise the system will show an ANR dialog. For longer work triggered by a broadcast, delegate immediately to WorkManager or a coroutine.

sections.heading: Common System Broadcast Events
items: Intent.ACTION_BOOT_COMPLETED — device has finished booting; requires RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETED permission
Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_LOW — battery has dropped to a critically low level
Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_OKAY — battery has recovered from low level
Intent.ACTION_POWER_CONNECTED — device has been plugged into a charger
Intent.ACTION_POWER_DISCONNECTED — device has been unplugged from charger
ConnectivityManager.CONNECTIVITY_ACTION — network connectivity has changed (deprecated for apps targeting API 28+; use NetworkCallback instead)
Intent.ACTION_AIRPLANE_MODE_CHANGED — airplane mode has been toggled
Intent.ACTION_LOCALE_CHANGED — user has changed the device language
Intent.ACTION_TIME_CHANGED — system time has been set manually
Intent.ACTION_PACKAGE_ADDED / REMOVED — an app has been installed or uninstalled
Intent.ACTION_SCREEN_ON / ACTION_SCREEN_OFF — screen turned on or off (runtime registration only)
Intent.ACTION_HEADSET_PLUG — wired headset plugged or unplugged

rows.how: Declared in AndroidManifest.xml with <receiver> tag and <intent-filter>
rows.lifecycle: Receiver exists even when the app is not running — system creates it to deliver broadcasts
rows.use_for: Boot completed, package installed/removed, system events that must wake the app
rows.limitations: Most implicit broadcasts are blocked for API 26+ (Oreo) due to background execution limits

rows.how: Registered in code with context.registerReceiver() and unregistered with unregisterReceiver()
rows.lifecycle: Only active while the registering component (Activity, Fragment, Service) is alive
rows.use_for: Screen on/off, battery changes, connectivity — events only relevant while app is in foreground
rows.limitations: Receiver is destroyed when the component unregisters — cannot receive broadcasts when app is closed

sections.heading: Creating a BroadcastReceiver
sections.content: A BroadcastReceiver is created by extending the BroadcastReceiver abstract class and overriding the onReceive(context, intent) method. The context passed to onReceive() should never be used to start an asynchronous task that outlives onReceive() itself — if you need to do longer work, use goAsync() to get a PendingResult that keeps the receiver alive, or better yet, enqueue a WorkManager task immediately. The receiver's class must be public for static registration to work, as the system instantiates it via reflection.

sections.heading: BroadcastReceiver Implementation Pattern
items: Extend BroadcastReceiver: class MyReceiver : BroadcastReceiver() { override fun onReceive(context: Context, intent: Intent) { } }
Check the action: when (intent.action) { Intent.ACTION_BOOT_COMPLETED -> { } ConnectivityManager.CONNECTIVITY_ACTION -> { } }
Extract extras: val message = intent.getStringExtra("key")
For short work (< 10 seconds): execute directly in onReceive()
For longer work: immediately enqueue WorkManager: WorkManager.getInstance(context).enqueue(myWorkRequest)
Do NOT start AsyncTask, create threads, or launch coroutines directly — they may be killed before completing
Use goAsync() for slightly longer but still bounded work: val result = goAsync(); coroutineScope.launch { doWork(); result.finish() }
Never show a Dialog from onReceive() — receivers do not have a window token
Never bind to a Service from onReceive() — the binding is asynchronous and onReceive() may return first

rows.description: <receiver android:name=".MyReceiver" android:exported="false"> — exported="false" prevents other apps from sending broadcasts to your receiver
<intent-filter><action android:name="android.intent.action.BOOT_COMPLETED" /></intent-filter> — inside the <receiver> tag
<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETED" /> — required for BOOT_COMPLETED broadcast
android:permission="com.example.MY_PERMISSION" on the <receiver> tag — only senders with this permission can trigger it
Check intent.action and handle accordingly; enqueue WorkManager for any non-trivial work

sections.heading: Dynamic Registration and Unregistration
items: Create receiver instance: val receiver = MyBroadcastReceiver()
Create IntentFilter: val filter = IntentFilter(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_LOW).apply { addAction(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_OKAY) }
Register in onStart(): registerReceiver(receiver, filter) — or ContextCompat.registerReceiver() on API 33+
Unregister in onStop(): unregisterReceiver(receiver) — always pair register/unregister to avoid leaks
For API 33+, specify export flag: ContextCompat.registerReceiver(this, receiver, filter, ContextCompat.RECEIVER_NOT_EXPORTED)
RECEIVER_NOT_EXPORTED — only your app can send broadcasts to this receiver
RECEIVER_EXPORTED — any app can send broadcasts to this receiver
In a Fragment: register in onViewCreated() / onStart() and unregister in onDestroyView() / onStop()
In Compose: use DisposableEffect to register and unregister with the composable lifecycle

rows.description: Apps targeting API 26+ cannot register most implicit broadcasts in the manifest — they are silently ignored
rows.exceptions: A small set of broadcasts are still deliverable: BOOT_COMPLETED, LOCKED_BOOT_COMPLETED, PACKAGE_REPLACED, MY_PACKAGE_REPLACED, etc.

rows.description: Broadcasts sent directly to your app's receiver (by component name) still work with manifest registration
rows.exceptions: Always works — these are targeted specifically at your receiver

rows.description: Dynamically registered receivers work normally for any broadcast action — not subject to implicit broadcast restrictions
rows.exceptions: None — runtime registration is the recommended approach for most broadcasts

rows.description: For events previously handled via implicit broadcasts, use: JobScheduler, WorkManager with constraints, or NetworkCallback for connectivity changes
rows.exceptions: Connectivity: use ConnectivityManager.registerNetworkCallback() instead of CONNECTIVITY_ACTION broadcast

sections.heading: Sending Custom Broadcasts
sections.content: In addition to receiving system broadcasts, your app can send its own custom broadcasts — either to itself or to other apps. Local broadcasts (within your own app) should use LocalBroadcastManager (now deprecated) or modern alternatives like SharedFlow in a ViewModel or EventBus. For inter-app broadcasts, use sendBroadcast() with an explicit component name or a custom permission to restrict who can receive it. Always use explicit intents or permission-restricted broadcasts for security — never send sensitive data in a broadcast that any app could intercept.

sections.heading: Sending Broadcasts
items: Send a global broadcast: sendBroadcast(Intent("com.example.MY_CUSTOM_ACTION"))
Send with data: val intent = Intent("com.example.ACTION").apply { putExtra("key", value) }; sendBroadcast(intent)
Restrict to receivers with a permission: sendBroadcast(intent, "com.example.MY_PERMISSION")
Send an ordered broadcast: sendOrderedBroadcast(intent, null) — receivers are called in priority order
Send explicitly to a specific receiver: intent.component = ComponentName(packageName, "com.example.MyReceiver"); sendBroadcast(intent)
LocalBroadcastManager is deprecated — use SharedFlow or EventBus for in-process events instead
For in-app communication: prefer LiveData, SharedFlow, or StateFlow over broadcasts entirely
System broadcasts (like ACTION_BATTERY_LOW) cannot be sent by apps — they are system-protected

rows.description: A broadcast delivered to one receiver at a time, in priority order — each receiver can pass data to the next or abort the broadcast
Sends an ordered broadcast; receivers are invoked in descending order of their declared android:priority in the intent-filter
Pass data to the next receiver in the chain: setResult(resultCode, resultData, resultExtras)
Read the data set by the previous receiver in the chain
Prevents lower-priority receivers from receiving the broadcast — only works in ordered broadcasts
<intent-filter android:priority="100"> — higher number = higher priority; default is 0; range is -1000 to 1000

sections.heading: Boot Completed Receiver — Common Pattern
items: Register in manifest with BOOT_COMPLETED action and RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETED permission
android:directBootAware="true" — allows receipt before the user unlocks the device after reboot (direct boot mode)
Use LOCKED_BOOT_COMPLETED for direct boot aware receivers — fires before user authentication
In onReceive(), re-schedule WorkManager periodic tasks that were cleared when the app was killed before reboot
In onReceive(), restart foreground services that need to run after boot
Keep onReceive() fast — enqueue WorkManager immediately and return
BOOT_COMPLETED is still delivered on API 26+ via manifest registration — it is one of the exempt broadcasts
Do not perform heavy initialization in a boot receiver — defer to WorkManager or lazy initialization

rows.description: Register for ConnectivityManager.CONNECTIVITY_ACTION in manifest — blocked on API 26+ for background apps
Register a NetworkCallback with ConnectivityManager for precise, lifecycle-aware connectivity monitoring
Use NetworkType.CONNECTED constraint in WorkManager — work runs when network is available without manual monitoring
Wrap NetworkCallback in a callbackFlow for reactive connectivity state that integrates with coroutines
rows.code: callbackFlow { registerNetworkCallback(request, object : NetworkCallback() { override fun onAvailable(n) { trySend(true) } }); awaitClose { unregisterNetworkCallback(this) } }

sections.heading: Security Best Practices for Broadcasts
items: Use android:exported="false" on <receiver> in the manifest unless other apps need to send broadcasts to it
For API 33+ dynamic registration, always specify RECEIVER_NOT_EXPORTED unless cross-app communication is required
Use custom permissions to restrict who can send broadcasts to your receiver: android:permission on <receiver>
Use custom permissions to restrict who can receive your broadcasts: pass permissionString to sendBroadcast()
Never include sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII) in a broadcast Intent — other apps with matching intent-filters could receive it
Use explicit intents (specify component name) when communicating within your own app
Validate the intent action and extras in onReceive() — malicious apps could send spoofed broadcasts
For in-app events, avoid broadcasts entirely — use SharedFlow, StateFlow, or LiveData

rows.description: onReceive() must complete within ~10 seconds; work that takes longer causes ANR
Extends the receiver's lifetime beyond onReceive() by returning a PendingResult token that must be finished explicitly
val pendingResult = goAsync(); coroutineScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { try { doWork() } finally { pendingResult.finish() } }
goAsync() extends the deadline but does not remove it — still subject to background process killing
For work exceeding a few seconds, enqueue WorkManager immediately and return — more robust than goAsync()

sections.heading: Broadcast Receiver in Compose with DisposableEffect
items: Use DisposableEffect to register and unregister a BroadcastReceiver with the composable's lifecycle
val context = LocalContext.current

DisposableEffect(context) { val receiver = MyBroadcastReceiver(); val filter = IntentFilter(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_LOW); context.registerReceiver(receiver, filter); onDispose { context.unregisterReceiver(receiver) } }
This ensures the receiver is unregistered when the composable leaves the composition — no leaks
The DisposableEffect key should be context or a stable value — changing the key unregisters and re-registers
Combine with a state variable to expose broadcast data to the composable: var batteryLow by remember { mutableStateOf(false) }
Set state inside onReceive(): batteryLow = true — triggers recomposition to update UI
Use this pattern for battery, screen on/off, headset plug, and other UI-relevant system events

rows.mistake: Performing long-running work directly in onReceive()
rows.fix: Enqueue WorkManager immediately in onReceive() and return — never block the main thread beyond a few milliseconds

rows.mistake: Registering a manifest receiver for implicit broadcasts targeting API 26+
rows.fix: Most implicit broadcasts are blocked — use dynamic registration or WorkManager constraints instead

rows.mistake: Forgetting to unregister a dynamically registered receiver
rows.fix: Always pair registerReceiver() in onStart() with unregisterReceiver() in onStop() to prevent memory leaks
LocalBroadcastManager is deprecated — use SharedFlow, StateFlow, LiveData, or an event bus for in-app communication

rows.mistake: Sending sensitive data in a broadcast Intent
rows.fix: Any app with a matching intent-filter can receive your broadcast — never include tokens, passwords, or PII

rows.mistake: Not declaring android:exported on receiver in API 33+
rows.fix: API 33+ requires explicit exported declaration — add android:exported="false" or "true" to avoid build errors

rows.mistake: Showing a Dialog or Toast from onReceive() in a static receiver
rows.fix: Static receivers have no UI context — use a notification instead, or send a broadcast to a running Activity

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Broadcast Receivers are Android's publish-subscribe mechanism for responding to system and app events. Use static manifest registration for critical system events like BOOT_COMPLETED that must wake the app, and dynamic runtime registration for UI-relevant events like battery level and screen state. Always keep onReceive() fast — delegate any real work to WorkManager immediately. For connectivity monitoring, replace the deprecated CONNECTIVITY_ACTION with NetworkCallback or WorkManager constraints. Secure your receivers with android:exported="false" and custom permissions, never send sensitive data through broadcasts, and replace LocalBroadcastManager with SharedFlow or StateFlow for all in-app event communication.
